session.tool_result: 600→ repo_config = load_config(main_root) 601→ # Load per-user local config (.erk/config.local.toml) and merge 602→ user_local_config = load_local_config(main_root) 603→ local_config = merge_configs_with_local( 604→ base_config=repo_config, 605→ local_config=user_local_config, 606→ ) 607→ 608→ # 8. Create GitHub-related classes (need repo_info, local_config) 609→ # Create issues first, then compose into github 610→ # Use plans_repo for cross-repo plan issue management if configured 611→ issues: GitHubIssues = RealGitHubIssues(target_repo=local_config.plans_repo, time=time) 612→ github: GitHub = RealGitHub(time, repo_info, issues=issues) 613→ 614→ plan_store: PlanStore = PlannedPRBackend(github, issues, time=RealTime()) 615→ plan_list_service: PlanListService = PlannedPRPlanListService(github) 616→ 617→ # Objectives are always issue-based regardless of plan backend 618→ objective_list_service: ObjectiveListService = RealObjectiveListService(github, issues) 619→ 620→ # 9. Apply dry-run wrappers if needed 621→ # Note: DryRunGitHub composes DryRunGitHubIssues internally, 622→ # but we still wrap issues separately for ctx.issues backward compatibility 623→ # Note: DryRunGitHub composes DryRunGitHubIssues internally for github.issues 624→ if dry_run: 625→ git = DryRunGit(git) 626→ graphite = DryRunGraphite(graphite) 627→ if graphite_branch_ops is not None: 628→ graphite_branch_ops = DryRunGraphiteBranchOps(graphite_branch_ops) 629→ github = DryRunGitHub(github)

Returns 'main' as final fallback 169→ if neither branch exists.

packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/branch_ops/abc.py:207: Uses git for-each-ref to batch-fetch upstream tracking information. packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/branch_ops/real.py:271: """Get sync status for all local branches via git for-each-ref.""" packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/gateway/git/branch_ops/real.py:275: "for-each-ref",

265→ ahead = int(parts[1]) 266→ return ahead, behind 267→ 268→ return 0, 0 269→ 270→ def get_all_branch_sync_info(self, repo_root: Path) -> dict[str, BranchSyncInfo]: 271→ """Get sync status for all local branches via git for-each-ref.""" 272→ result = subprocess.run( 273→ [ 274→ "git", 275→ "for-each-ref", 276→ "--format=%(refname:short)\t%(upstream:short)\t%(upstream:track)", 277→ "refs/heads/", 278→ ], 279→ cwd=repo_root, 280→ capture_output=True, 281→ text=True, 282→ check=False, 283→ ) 284→ 285→ if result.returncode != 0: 286→ return {} 287→ 288→ sync_info: dict[str, BranchSyncInfo] = {} 289→ for line in result.stdout.strip().split("\n"): 290→ if not line: 291→ continue 292→ parts = line.split("\t") 293→ branch = parts[0] 294→ upstream = parts[1] if len(parts) > 1 and parts[1] else None

75→# (error: "master already used by worktree"). Instead, we delete the remote 76→# branch separately via REST API after the merge succeeds. 77→USE_GH_PR_MERGE_FOR_LANDING = True 78→ 79→ 80→class RealGitHub(GitHub): 81→ """Production implementation using gh CLI. 82→ 83→ All GitHub operations execute actual gh commands via subprocess. 84→ """ 85→ 86→ def __init__( 87→ self, 88→ time: Time, 89→ repo_info: RepoInfo | None, 90→ *, 91→ issues: GitHubIssues, 92→ ): 93→ """Initialize RealGitHub. 94→ 95→ Args: 96→ time: Time abstraction for sleep operations 97→ repo_info: Repository owner/name info (None if not in a GitHub repo) 98→ issues: GitHubIssues gateway for issue operations 99→ """ 100→ self._time = time 101→ self._repo_info = repo_info 102→ self._issues = issues 103→ self._default_branch_cache: dict[Path, str] = {} 104→
session.thinking: Now let me look at the `GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY` fully and understand the HttpClient's context in plan data provider to design my plan:
session.tool_result:
164→# Query for draft plan PRs with rich details (checks, review threads, mergeability) 165→# Used by PlannedPRPlanListService for single-call data fetching. 166→# Note: pullRequests connection does not support filterBy for creator or draft status, 167→# so those filters must be applied client-side. 168→GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY = """query( 169→ $owner: String! 170→ $repo: String! 171→ $labels: [String!]! 172→ $states: [PullRequestState!]! 173→ $first: Int! 174→) { 175→ repository(owner: $owner, name: $repo) { 176→ pullRequests( 177→ labels: $labels 178→ states: $states 179→ first: $first 180→ orderBy: {field: UPDATED_AT, direction: DESC} 181→ ) { 182→ nodes { 183→ number 184→ title 185→ body 186→ state 187→ url 188→ isDraft 189→ author { login } 190→ labels(first: 100) { nodes { name } } 191→ createdAt 192→ updatedAt 193→ headRefName
... [22 lines omitted] ...

1→"""GitHub authentication token retrieval. 2→ 3→This module provides functions to fetch GitHub tokens via the gh CLI, 4→designed to be called once at startup to avoid repeated subprocess overhead. 5→""" 6→ 7→from erk_shared.subprocess_utils import run_subprocess_with_context 8→ 9→ 10→def fetch_github_token() -> str: 11→ """Fetch GitHub token via gh CLI. 12→ 13→ This should be called once at startup and cached. The token can then 14→ be used to create RealHttpClient instances for direct API calls. 15→ 16→ Returns: 17→ GitHub authentication token 18→ 19→ Raises: 20→ RuntimeError: If gh auth token fails 21→ ValueError: If token is empty 22→ """ 23→ result = run_subprocess_with_context( 24→ cmd=["gh", "auth", "token", "--hostname", "github.com"], 25→ operation_context="fetch GitHub token for github.com", 26→ ) 27→ token = result.stdout.strip() 28→ if not token: 29→ msg = "Empty token returned from gh auth for github.com" 30→ raise ValueError(msg)
... [2 lines omitted] ...
session.thinking:
Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me now design the plan.
The key bottleneck: `gh api` subprocess calls are extremely slow. Each `gh api` call involves: 1. Process fork/exec (~10ms) 2. Go runtime startup (~100-200ms) 3. TLS handshake (~200-300ms) 4. HTTP request (~200-1000ms) 5. Total: ~500ms-1.5s per call
Current flow for PlannedPR plans: 1. REST `gh api repos/.../issues?...` (subprocess) → ~500-1500ms 2. GraphQL `gh api graphql` enrichment (subprocess) → ~500-1500ms
3. `git worktree list` (subprocess) → ~10-20ms 4. N `git rev-parse` for Graphite branches → ~5-10ms * N 5. Row building (in-memory) → ~50-100ms
The HttpClient already exists and uses httpx, injected into `RealPlanDataProvider`, with the GitHub token fetched once at startup. The cleanest solution is to add a direct HTTP method to `PlannedPRPlanListService` that uses `HttpClient.get()` for REST calls and handles list responses, bypassing the subprocess overhead entirely.
The issue is that `HttpClient.get()` returns `dict[str, Any]` but the REST endpoint returns a list, so I need to either change the return type to `Any`, add a `get_list()` method, or use httpx directly—option B preserves type safety best. Since the service doesn't have HttpClient access yet, I can either inject it or handle the fetch in the data provider instead, with the latter being simpler.
Looking at this differently, the service calls `list_plan_prs_with_details()` which already handles the REST/GraphQL merging and parsing logic. Rather than duplicating that parsing in the data provider, I should keep the service's existing method and just optimize the HTTP calls within it—the parsing logic in `_parse_plan_prs_with_details()` already handles converting the raw response into the needed structures.
So my approach is to add a `graphql()` method to HttpClient that handles POST requests to the GraphQL endpoint, then pass the HttpClient to PlannedPRPlanListService so it can execute the query directly via httpx instead of subprocess, while reusing the existing parser.
But wait, that parser lives on `RealGitHub` and isn't accessible from the service layer. I'd need to extract it first.
Actually, the simpler path is to just inject the HttpClient into `RealGitHub` itself and use it within `list_plan_prs_with_details`. That way I can keep the parser where it is and avoid restructuring multiple layers.
Adding http_client as an optional parameter...
Checking if http_client is available and routing to the appropriate implementation...
This keeps changes minimal—just `RealGitHub`, context setup, and the factory methods. The existing parsing logic stays intact, and the TUI gets the performance benefit immediately since it already has the token.
Actually, I don't need the HttpClient abstraction here at all. Since this is already the real implementation, I can use httpx directly inside `list_plan_prs_with_details` and grab the token from the auth module. The main question is just making sure the token gets passed through properly.
The cleaner approach is to go back to a single GraphQL query instead of splitting between REST and GraphQL—client-side filtering is fast enough, so there's no need for the server-side creator filtering that required the split in the first place. enough when using httpx)
Actually, even with httpx, two calls (REST + GraphQL) would share a connection but still be 2 round-trips. A single GraphQL call is 1 round-trip. So reverting to the single query is better.
But the plan mentioned that `GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY` uses `pullRequests` which doesn't support `filterBy` for creator. That means client-side author filtering. That's fine since we're doing all filtering in-memory anyway.
Here's my refined plan:
**Option A: Revert to single GraphQL + httpx** - Pros: 1 round-trip, simpler code - Cons: No server-side creator filtering (but this filter may not help much)
**Option B: Keep REST+GraphQL split + httpx** - Pros: Server-side filtering - Cons: 2 round-trips (though with connection reuse, TLS is shared)
I think Option A is better. Now I need to consider which parsing function to use—`_parse_plan_prs_with_details` handles the single-query response while `_merge_rest_graphql_pr_data` handles the merged format, but both produce the same output types, so either approach works.
My final plan is to add httpx support to RealGitHub, then revert `list_plan_prs_with_details` to use a single GraphQL query executed via httpx with the existing `GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY`, reusing the `_parse_plan_prs_with_details` parser, and keeping client-side author filtering.
I'll also optimize Graphite's `get_all_branches` by replacing multiple `git rev-parse` calls with a single `git for-each-ref` command to reduce subprocess overhead, then thread the token through the context factories in both `src/erk/core/context.py` and the shared factories.
For `exclude_labels`, GraphQL's `pullRequests` doesn't support an `excludeLabels` parameter, so I'll need to apply that filtering in Python after parsing the results.
These changes should cut the total time from around 6.6 seconds down to 2-3 seconds—the biggest win comes from eliminating the extra httpx call, with additional savings from the consolidated git command.
Now I'm checking whether `get_workflow_runs_by_node_ids` needs optimization too. The Plans tab skips it with `skip_workflow_runs=True`, but I should verify how it's called elsewhere...
Looking at the code, workflow runs are only fetched when `show_runs` is true or `run_state` is set, both of which default to false. So for the default view, that call doesn't even happen. The optimization path is solid—just the single GraphQL call plus local processing.
Now I'm checking how the HTTP client and token are being passed around in the context to make sure I'm using the right approach for the API call.

session.thinking:
Good. Now I have the full picture. Let me now look at where the token is already available - the TUI path creates a RealPlanDataProvider in list_cmd.py with a RealHttpClient using a fetched token. So the token is already fetched. The question is how to pass it down to the GitHub gateway.
Let me now synthesize everything and write the plan. I have enough context now.
The key approach:
**1. Revert to single GraphQL query, executed via httpx instead of `gh api` subprocess**
This means: - In `PlannedPRPlanListService`, instead of calling `self._github.list_plan_prs_with_details()` (which uses `execute_gh_command_with_retry`), execute the GraphQL query directly via HttpClient
- I can reuse the existing `GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY` and the parsing logic from `RealGitHub._parse_plan_prs_with_details()`, which means either extracting that parsing into a standalone function or having the GitHub gateway support httpx directly. Option (b) is the cleanest approach - keep calling through the gateway but add httpx support to it.
Now I'm thinking about the simplest path forward. The current implementation does a REST call to get the issue/PR list, then a GraphQL enrichment call, and merges the results. I could either replace both subprocess calls with httpx while keeping that two-step flow, or switch to a single GraphQL query using the existing query. The single GraphQL approach seems more efficient.
So the plan is to add an optional `http_client` parameter to `RealGitHub`, and when it's available, execute the GraphQL query directly through httpx instead of subprocess. The response parsing already exists, so I just need to wire up the HTTP call and apply the filters client-side.
Now I'm realizing the context factory at line 612 doesn't have access to the token yet—that's fetched later in the flow. I need to figure out how to pass the http_client through the initialization chain. The issue is that the context factory creates the service before the HTTP client is available, but the client is only ready later in the CLI command layer. The simplest solution is to fetch the GitHub token directly in the context factory using a subprocess call, which is fast and only happens once at startup, then create the HTTP client there.
However, I need to consider that not every code path needs GitHub API access—some might work offline. Since the context factory already creates RealGitHub which makes API calls, fetching the token should be safe here, but I should handle the case where token fetch fails gracefully by making it optional.
Still writing error handling... I'm realizing this approach of threading the http_client through as a parameter to the service method is cleaner since it avoids modifying the context factories entirely. The tradeoff is that I'd need to update the PlanListService ABC and all its implementations to accept this optional parameter, but that's a more localized change than restructuring the dependency injection.
Looking at the implementations, there are four total: the ABC itself, PlannedPRPlanListService, RealPlanListService, and FakePlanListService. Adding an optional parameter follows the existing pattern since the ABC already has optional parameters like exclude_labels, so this is consistent with how the code evolves.
Now I'm thinking through what the single GraphQL query approach would look like...
I'd execute the query through the HTTP client with the query string and variables, then parse the response. The parsing logic already exists in RealGitHub's method that converts the GraphQL response into the tuple of PR details and pull request info. Rather than duplicating that logic in the service, I should extract it into a standalone function that both the gateway and service can use—probably in the GitHub module or a dedicated parsing utility.
But those parsing methods depend on other helper methods from RealGitHub like status rollup and mergeable status parsing, which are also used elsewhere. Instead of untangling all those dependencies, the simpler approach is to just add an optional HTTP client parameter to the existing gateway method so it can use a custom client when needed.
Adding optional parameter to method signature... I need to update all five gateway implementations to support the http_client parameter, which feels like a lot of boilerplate. Let me reconsider—actually, the cleanest approach is to implement the fast fetch directly in the plan data provider itself, since it already has access to the http_client, and bypass the service layer entirely when that's available.
The parsing logic I need is already in the real GitHub implementation, so I can extract that into a reusable function rather than duplicating it.
But the service layer does more than just parse—it converts the raw PR details into Plan objects and handles workflow run fetching. If I bypass the service entirely and implement this in the data provider, I'd need to replicate all that transformation logic myself, which defeats the purpose of keeping things simple. I'm seeing there's some duplication with the existing service, but the response parsing is the main piece. Actually, I'm wondering if I should just modify `PlannedPRPlanListService` to accept an HttpClient directly instead of relying on the GitHub instance, since the service already has that dependency available.
Modifying the service initialization...
I'm realizing the context factory gets called from multiple places, and not all of them have GitHub authentication available, so passing the HTTP client through there could be problematic. Let me step back and think about this more carefully—I should check how the context factory is actually used and whether the token is already being fetched somewhere nearby. Actually, I've been overthinking the design here. Let me just commit to an approach: I'll extract the parsing logic from RealGitHub into a standalone function, then add an optional HTTP client parameter to PlannedPRPlanListService and thread it through ErkContext.
Now I'm settling on the simplest path forward: just add the HTTP client to RealGitHub's constructor and use it in the parsing method when available. I need to update the RealGitHub constructor, modify the parsing logic to use httpx for the GraphQL query when the client is present, and handle the test factories accordingly. I'm looking at where to instantiate the http_client at those construction sites—the first two locations can fetch the token and create it, while the other two can just pass None as the default. For the `get_all_branches` fix, I'll update the RealGraphite method to use `git for-each-ref` instead. This keeps the changes minimal across just a few files.
Now examining the actual production code locations where RealGitHub gets instantiated—they're spread across the context factory, the gateway, and the statusline. The key insight is that I only need to add http_client where the TUI or dashboard is actually used, and the main context factory in `src/erk/core/context.py` is what powers `erk dash`. Let me trace through list_cmd.py to see where the token gets fetched and confirm the flow.
I see the pattern now—the token is fetched at the CLI command level, then an http_client is created and passed directly to the provider. This means I have three options: add token fetching to the context factory itself, thread the http_client through multiple layers of dependency injection, or mutate RealGitHub after construction. Each has tradeoffs I need to weigh.
Option 1 seems cleanest since adding a subprocess call to fetch the token is only about 50ms, but the context factory runs for every erk command, not just the ones that need GitHub. And if the gh CLI isn't installed, the token fetch will fail even for commands that don't require it.
So I'm going with option 2 instead: pass the http_client directly to the service constructor and create it in the context factory with a try-catch around the token fetch, so if authentication fails the service just gets a null client and handles it gracefully. I'm outlining the implementation strategy: modify `PlannedPRPlanListService` to optionally accept an HTTP client for direct GraphQL queries, extract the parsing logic into a reusable function, set up token fetching and HTTP client creation in the context factory, and switch `get_all_branches` to use `git for-each-ref`. Now I need to trace through how `get_plan_list_data` is invoked from the data provider to ensure the integration point is correct. I need to map the REST parameters to GraphQL query variables—`state` converts to a `$states` array format, `labels` and `limit` map directly, and I'll handle `author` and `exclude_labels` filtering on the client side. I should also verify the state mapping since REST uses lowercase "open"/"closed" while GraphQL expects uppercase ["OPEN"]/["CLOSED"], and check how the existing parser handles this.
Now I'm looking at the parsing logic to understand what I'd need to extract or rewrite—the main `_parse_plan_prs_with_details` method is around 115 lines and depends on a few helper methods for status rollup, mergeable status, and review thread counts. Let me examine those dependencies to see how much code I actually need to refactor. I'm realizing the cleanest path forward is to extract these parsing utilities into standalone functions so the service can reuse them without duplicating code or creating circular dependencies. Let me just sketch out the implementation plan and move forward.
Actually, I can simplify this by having the service call `self._github.list_plan_prs_with_details()` and letting `RealGitHub` handle the httpx logic internally—that way I avoid the extraction work entirely. I'd add an optional `http_client` parameter to `RealGitHub.__init__`, use it in the method when available to make a single GraphQL query, and then pass it through at the construction sites, particularly in the TUI context setup. I also need to update a few other places where `RealGraphite` and `RealBranchOps` are instantiated to use the optimized `git for-each-ref` approach for fetching all branches at once instead of making individual queries. For the remaining construction sites that don't have the http_client available, they'll just keep the default subprocess behavior. The existing tests should be fine since they use `FakeGitHub` which has its own implementation that won't be affected by these changes.

session.assistant:
Here's the plan:
---
# Fix: Actually speed up `erk dash` Plans/Learn tabs
## Context
The previous PR (#8168) attempted to speed up `erk dash` by replacing a single GraphQL query with a REST+GraphQL two-step approach. This **made things worse** — still 6.6s. Root cause analysis reveals:
1. **`gh api` subprocess overhead is the dominant cost.** Each `gh api` call spawns a Go process (~200-300ms for Go runtime + TLS handshake) on top of network latency. The previous change went from **1 subprocess call to 2**, adding overhead instead of removing it. 2. **Graphite `get_all_branches()` runs N sequential `git rev-parse` subprocess calls** — one per tracked branch. With 30-50 branches, this adds ~150-500ms.
**Expected improvement**: from ~6.6s to ~2-3s by eliminating subprocess overhead and reducing API round-trips.
## Approach: Replace `gh api` subprocess with httpx + fix Graphite branch cache
### Step 1: Add `http_client` to `RealGitHub` constructor (impl detail, no ABC change) ### Step 2: Revert to single GraphQL query (`GET_PLAN_PRS_WITH_DETAILS_QUERY`) via httpx — 1 HTTP call instead of 2 subprocess calls ### Step 3: Fetch token and create HttpClient in context factory (`context.py`) ### Step 4: Fix Graphite `get_all_branches` — single `git for-each-ref` instead of N `git rev-parse` (gateway 5-place update)
---

tool_use.name: AskUserQuestion
tool_use.param: [{'question': '📋 Fix: Actually speed up `erk dash` Plans/Learn tabs\nCurrent context: (wt:...[truncated 499 chars]...ion': 'Open plan in editor to review or modify before deciding.'}], 'multiSelect': False}]

session.tool_result: The user doesn't want to proceed with this tool use. The tool use was rejected (eg. if it was a file edit, the new_string was NOT written to the file). STOP what you are doing and wait for the user to tell you how to proceed.
session.user: [Request interrupted by user for tool use]

2. Graphite get_all_branches() runs N sequential git rev-parse subprocess calls — one per tracked branch. With 30-50 branches, this adds ~150-500ms.
make a plan for this is interesting
session.tool_result:
130→ # parse_graphite_pr_info expects JSON string, so convert back 131→ return parse_graphite_pr_info(json.dumps(data)) 132→ 133→ def get_all_branches(self, git_ops: Git, repo_root: Path) -> dict[str, BranchMetadata]: 134→ """Get all gt-tracked branches with metadata. 135→ 136→ Reads .git/.graphite_cache_persist and enriches with commit SHAs from git. 137→ Returns empty dict if cache doesn't exist or git operations fail. 138→ 139→ Results are cached based on file mtime - the cache is automatically 140→ invalidated when the underlying file changes, whether from erk operations 141→ or external gt commands. 142→ """ 143→ git_dir = git_ops.repo.get_git_common_dir(repo_root) 144→ if git_dir is None: 145→ return {} 146→ 147→ cache_file = git_dir / ".graphite_cache_persist" 148→ if not cache_file.exists(): 149→ return {} 150→ 151→ # Check if cache is still valid via mtime 152→ current_mtime = cache_file.stat().st_mtime 153→ if ( 154→ self._branches_cache is not None 155→ and self._branches_cache_mtime is not None 156→ and self._branches_cache_mtime == current_mtime 157→ ): 158→ return self._branches_cache 159→
... [20 lines omitted] ...

Returns 'main' as final fallback 150→ if neither branch exists. 151→ 152→ Args: 153→ repo_root: Path to the repository root 154→
